items.prompt_md: In the BFS right-side-view solution, why does the loop snapshot `level_size = len(queue)` before the inner `for` loop runs?
options: It freezes how many nodes belong to the current level, before their children get enqueued into the same queue, so `i == level_size - 1` reliably marks the last node of THIS level
It avoids an infinite loop, since the queue would otherwise never empty
It sorts the nodes within a level from right to left
It reduces the algorithm's time complexity from O(n^2) to O(n)
items.answer: It freezes how many nodes belong to the current level, before their children get enqueued into the same queue, so `i == level_size - 1` reliably marks the last node of THIS level
items.explanation_md: The inner loop enqueues the next level's children as it runs, so without snapshotting the count first, there'd be no fixed boundary telling you which dequeued node is really the last one on the current level.

items.prompt_md: In the DFS version of right-side view, `dfs` visits the right child before the left child, and only appends a value when `depth == len(result)`. Why does visiting right first matter?
options: It guarantees the first node DFS reaches at any given depth is the rightmost node at that depth
It makes the recursion terminate faster than visiting left first
It is required so the base case (`node is None`) is reached correctly
It has no effect — visiting left first would produce the same result
items.answer: It guarantees the first node DFS reaches at any given depth is the rightmost node at that depth
items.explanation_md: Because right is explored before left, DFS always arrives at a depth's rightmost node before any node further left at that same depth — so 'first node seen at this depth' is exactly the rightmost one.

items.prompt_md: Why does the condition `depth == len(result)` correctly identify 'the first node seen at this depth' in the DFS right-side-view approach?
options: Because `result` gains one entry per depth level visited so far, so its length equals the next unvisited depth — the first time DFS reaches a new depth, this equality holds
Because `depth` and `len(result)` are always mathematically identical for any binary tree
Because it checks whether the current node is a leaf
Because it counts how many times the function has recursed so far
items.answer: Because `result` gains one entry per depth level visited so far, so its length equals the next unvisited depth — the first time DFS reaches a new depth, this equality holds
items.explanation_md: `result` only grows when a new depth is recorded, so its current length is exactly the index of the next depth still unseen; the first call that reaches that depth satisfies `depth == len(result)`.

items.prompt_md: How does the 'good nodes' problem's running maximum differ in direction from a subtree aggregate like tree depth?
options: The running maximum flows top-down as an argument passed into each recursive call, while a subtree aggregate like depth flows bottom-up as a return value combined at the parent
Both flow in the same direction; the only difference is which operator (max vs. addition) is used
The running maximum is computed after the full traversal finishes, using a second pass
Depth is computed top-down using an argument, while good-node counting is computed bottom-up using a return value
items.answer: The running maximum flows top-down as an argument passed into each recursive call, while a subtree aggregate like depth flows bottom-up as a return value combined at the parent
items.explanation_md: Good-node counting needs to know the max-so-far along the path FROM THE ROOT, so that state must travel down through recursive arguments; depth instead combines children's already-computed values back up to the parent.

items.prompt_md: In `good_nodes`, why is `max(max_so_far, root.val)` passed to BOTH the left and right recursive calls, rather than each side getting its own independently threaded maximum?
options: Because both children sit on the same root-to-node path prefix (through the current node), so they must inherit the same updated running maximum from that shared ancestor
Because Python requires both recursive calls to receive identical arguments
Because the left and right subtrees are always symmetric in value
Because it avoids recomputing `root.val` twice
items.answer: Because both children sit on the same root-to-node path prefix (through the current node), so they must inherit the same updated running maximum from that shared ancestor
items.explanation_md: Every path to either child passes through the current node, so both children's path-so-far maximum must include the current node's value — hence the same updated `new_max` is handed to both.

items.prompt_md: What's the key difference between a question asking for a per-level VIEW (like right-side view) and one asking for a per-level AGGREGATE (like the sum of each level's values)?
options: A view selects a single representative node per level (e.g., the last one seen), while an aggregate combines every node's value on the level into one number
A view requires DFS while an aggregate always requires BFS
A view only works on balanced trees, while an aggregate works on any tree
There is no meaningful difference — both are computed identically
items.answer: A view selects a single representative node per level (e.g., the last one seen), while an aggregate combines every node's value on the level into one number
items.explanation_md: A view narrows a level down to one chosen node's value (leftmost, rightmost, etc.); a level-sum aggregate instead combines all of that level's values — both can be computed with the same BFS level-order shape, just tracking different running state per level.

items.prompt_md: Why is 'counting good nodes' described as a TOP-DOWN path aggregate rather than a BOTTOM-UP subtree aggregate?
options: Because whether a node is 'good' depends only on information flowing down from the root (the max seen on the path so far), not on anything computed from its own descendants
Because the recursion always starts at the deepest leaf and works upward
Because it requires a second traversal after the first one completes
Because it can only be computed with an explicit stack, never recursion
items.answer: Because whether a node is 'good' depends only on information flowing down from the root (the max seen on the path so far), not on anything computed from its own descendants
items.explanation_md: A node's 'good' status depends on ancestors, which is known before you even look at its children — the opposite of a bottom-up aggregate like subtree height, which needs children's results first.

items.prompt_md: What's a common bug when implementing the BFS right-side-view approach?
options: Appending a node's value every time it's dequeued, instead of only when `i == level_size - 1`, which records every node on the level instead of just the rightmost
Enqueuing the left child before the right child
Using a `deque` instead of a plain Python list for the queue
Checking `node.left` and `node.right` for `None` before enqueuing them
items.answer: Appending a node's value every time it's dequeued, instead of only when `i == level_size - 1`, which records every node on the level instead of just the rightmost
items.explanation_md: Without gating the append on being the last node processed in the level's inner loop, the result ends up with every node's value instead of just one representative per level.

items.prompt_md: How would you adapt the right-side-view BFS approach to produce the LEFT side view instead?
options: Record the value at `i == 0` (the first node processed in the level) instead of `i == level_size - 1` (the last)
Enqueue right children before left children, but keep recording the last node per level
Reverse the final result list after the BFS completes
Switch from BFS to a min-heap ordered by node value
items.answer: Record the value at `i == 0` (the first node processed in the level) instead of `i == level_size - 1` (the last)
items.explanation_md: With children enqueued left-then-right, the first node dequeued at each level is the leftmost one, so gating the append on `i == 0` instead of the last index gives the left side view.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of both the top-down `good_nodes` DFS and the BFS right-side-view solution, on an n-node tree?
options: O(n) for both — each visits every node exactly once, doing O(1) work per node
O(n log n) for good_nodes, O(n) for right-side view
O(n^2) for good_nodes, since the running maximum must be recomputed at every node
O(n) for good_nodes, O(n^2) for right-side view, since every level's full list is rebuilt
items.answer: O(n) for both — each visits every node exactly once, doing O(1) work per node
items.explanation_md: Both are a single pass over the tree: good_nodes threads the running maximum down as an argument (no recomputation), and right-side view tracks only the last-popped index per BFS level — each is O(n) time.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the BFS right-side-view approach in the worst case, and what drives it?
options: O(n) in the worst case — the queue can hold an entire wide level, which for a full tree can be close to n/2 nodes
O(h) always, the same as the DFS version, since only one path is tracked at a time
O(1), since only the last node per level is ever stored
O(log n), because BFS always processes nodes in sorted order
items.answer: O(n) in the worst case — the queue can hold an entire wide level, which for a full tree can be close to n/2 nodes
items.explanation_md: The queue holds every node of the current level at once; a full tree's widest level holds close to n/2 nodes, giving O(n) space even though only the last value per level ends up in the output.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the DFS right-side-view approach (right-before-left), compared to the BFS version?
options: O(h) — just the recursion call stack, which is typically smaller than the BFS queue's O(n) worst case
O(n), identical to BFS, since both must store every level's values before choosing one
O(1), since DFS never uses any extra memory beyond the input tree
O(n^2), because DFS revisits nodes multiple times to determine depth
items.answer: O(h) — just the recursion call stack, which is typically smaller than the BFS queue's O(n) worst case
items.explanation_md: The DFS version's only extra memory is the recursion stack, bounded by height h, whereas BFS's queue can hold an entire wide level (up to ~n/2 nodes) — the two approaches trade off the same O(n) time for different space profiles.

items.prompt_md: You need to return the value visible from the right side of a binary tree at each depth (the rightmost node per level). What approach fits?
options: BFS by level, keeping only the last node dequeued at each level (or DFS visiting right before left, keeping only the first node seen at each depth)
In-order traversal, taking every third value
Recurse comparing the tree's left half against its right half node by node
Sort all node values and take the largest one at each depth
items.answer: BFS by level, keeping only the last node dequeued at each level (or DFS visiting right before left, keeping only the first node seen at each depth)
items.explanation_md: 'Visible from one side, per level' is the per-level-selection tell — BFS keep-last or right-first DFS keep-first both isolate one representative node per level.

items.prompt_md: You need to count how many nodes in a binary tree are 'good' — a node is good if its value is at least the maximum value seen so far on the path from the root down to it. Which approach fits?
options: DFS that carries the running maximum-so-far as an argument passed DOWN into each recursive call
Post-order DFS that returns the maximum of each subtree back UP to combine at the parent
BFS that only inspects the last node processed at each level
A hash map keyed by node value, checked for membership as you scan
items.answer: DFS that carries the running maximum-so-far as an argument passed DOWN into each recursive call
items.explanation_md: A root-to-node path property needs the accumulated state to flow top-down through the recursion, as a parameter each call passes to its children — the opposite direction from a bottom-up subtree aggregate.

items.prompt_md: You need the sum of all node values at each depth level of a binary tree, returned as a list. What approach fits?
options: BFS by level, summing all values dequeued during each level's inner loop
DFS visiting right before left, keeping only the first value seen at each depth
In-order traversal, tracking a running total that resets at each leaf
Recurse comparing corresponding nodes between the tree's left and right halves
items.answer: BFS by level, summing all values dequeued during each level's inner loop
items.explanation_md: A per-level aggregate (sum of the whole level) reuses the same BFS level-order shape as a per-level view, but combines every value on the level instead of picking just one.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the lowest common ancestor of two given nodes in a binary tree (not necessarily a BST). What approach fits?
options: DFS from the root; a node is the LCA if the target nodes are found in different subtrees below it (or it is one of the targets itself)
BFS by level, keeping only the last node seen per level
Sort the two target values and use two pointers to converge on their ancestor
Build a hash map of value-to-count and look for a shared key
items.answer: DFS from the root; a node is the LCA if the target nodes are found in different subtrees below it (or it is one of the targets itself)
items.explanation_md: LCA is an aggregate question answered by a single DFS pass that reports upward whether each subtree contains either target, converging on the split point where both targets diverge.

items.prompt_md: You need to count the total number of nodes in a binary tree. What's the best approach?
options: DFS (or BFS) visiting every node exactly once, summing 1 per node as the recursion/traversal combines results
In-order traversal, stopping as soon as a duplicate value is seen
Use BST ordering to prune the traversal to a single path
Two pointers converging from the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves
items.answer: DFS (or BFS) visiting every node exactly once, summing 1 per node as the recursion/traversal combines results
items.explanation_md: A total node count is the simplest possible tree aggregate — one full pass, no pruning possible, since every node contributes to the count regardless of value.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the two numbers in an unsorted array that add up to a given target. What's the best approach?
options: Use a hash map to record each value's index as you scan, checking for `target - value` seen so far
BFS by level, keeping the last value seen at each depth
DFS carrying a running sum down the recursion as an argument, as in the good-nodes pattern
Recurse comparing the array's first half against its second half
items.answer: Use a hash map to record each value's index as you scan, checking for `target - value` seen so far
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: there's no tree here — a flat array two-sum problem is solved with a hash map lookup for the complement, not any level-order or path-aggregate tree technique.

items.prompt_md: You need to detect whether a singly linked list contains a cycle. What's the best approach?
options: Use slow and fast pointers walking the list; if they ever meet, there's a cycle
BFS by level, keeping only the last node seen per level, as in a tree's right-side view
DFS carrying a running maximum down the list as an argument
Count all the 'nodes' the way you'd count all nodes in a tree
items.answer: Use slow and fast pointers walking the list; if they ever meet, there's a cycle
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a linked list has no levels or branching, so 'per-level view' and 'path aggregate' tree techniques don't apply — cycle detection here is the classic linked-list fast/slow pointer technique.

items.prompt_md: You need to evaluate a fully parenthesized arithmetic expression given as a string. What's the best approach?
options: Use a stack to hold operands and operators, popping and applying them as closing parentheses are encountered
BFS the expression string level by level, as if it were a tree
DFS the expression carrying a running sum down as an argument, as with good-nodes
Count the characters the way you'd count nodes in a tree
items.answer: Use a stack to hold operands and operators, popping and applying them as closing parentheses are encountered
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: expression evaluation with nested parentheses is a stack problem (LIFO resolution of the innermost group first) — there's no tree levels or root-to-node path here to exploit.

items.prompt_md: You need the average value of the nodes at each depth level of a binary tree, returned as a list. What approach fits?
options: BFS by level, summing all values and dividing by the level's node count during each level's inner loop
DFS visiting right before left, recording only the first value seen at each depth
In-order traversal, averaging every pair of adjacent values
Use BST ordering to prune the traversal to a single path
items.answer: BFS by level, summing all values and dividing by the level's node count during each level's inner loop
items.explanation_md: Per-level average is another per-level aggregate built on the same BFS level-order shape — sum and count are tracked while a level's nodes are dequeued, then divided once the level closes.

items.prompt_md: You need to know, for every node in a binary tree, the maximum value found anywhere in its own subtree (not the path from the root). What approach fits?
options: Bottom-up DFS that returns each subtree's max, combining children's results at the parent
Top-down DFS carrying a running maximum-so-far down from the root, as in good-nodes
BFS by level, keeping only the last node dequeued per level
Recurse comparing the tree against a mirrored copy of itself
items.answer: Bottom-up DFS that returns each subtree's max, combining children's results at the parent
items.explanation_md: A per-node subtree aggregate needs information flowing UP from descendants, the opposite direction from the top-down root-to-node path aggregates (like good-nodes) this lesson also covers — both are still single-pass DFS, but the state travels the other way.
